meta.notes: Belize is the only English-speaking country in Central America — a unique blend of Caribbean, Maya, Garifuna, Creole, and Mestizo cultures. World-class diving and snorkeling on the Mesoamerican Barrier Reef (second largest in the world), Maya ruins, and jungle. Small country — easy to cover in 1-2 weeks. Safety is location-dependent: tourist areas are safe, parts of Belize City require care.

language_and_money.english_proficiency: Very high — English is the official language and widely spoken throughout. Belizean Creole is the lingua franca. No language barrier for English speakers.
language_and_money.cash_vs_card: USD widely accepted everywhere at a fixed 2:1 rate (BZ$2 = US$1). Cards accepted at most hotels, dive shops, and restaurants on the cayes and in San Ignacio. Cash essential in rural areas, villages, and smaller establishments. Always confirm whether prices are in BZD or USD.
language_and_money.atm_availability: Available in Belize City, San Ignacio, Placencia, and Ambergris Caye (San Pedro). Limited on Caye Caulker and Tobacco Caye. Very scarce in remote jungle and southern areas. ATMs dispense BZD. Withdraw sufficient cash before heading to cayes or jungle lodges.
language_and_money.tipping_culture: Expected in tourist areas — US/Caribbean norms apply. 10-15% in restaurants, $5-10/day for dive guides, $10-15/day for tour guides. Lodges often add service charge.
language_and_money.tipping_etiquette: 10-15% in restaurants. $5-10/dive for dive masters. $10-15/day for guides. $2-3/bag for hotel porters. Some lodges include 10% service charge — check bill.

visa_requirements.us_citizen: No visa required for stays up to 30 days (extendable to 6 months)

entry_and_border.passport_validity_rule: Must be valid for the duration of stay plus 6 months
entry_and_border.departure_tax: BZ$37.50 (approximately US$18.75) — sometimes included in airline ticket
entry_and_border.conservation_fee: BZ$3.75 per day (US$1.88) — paid on departure, max BZ$75 total
entry_and_border.customs_restrictions: Standard restrictions on narcotics, firearms, and agricultural products. Pre-Columbian artifacts cannot be exported. Declaration required for cash over US$10,000.
entry_and_border.duty_free_allowances: 200 cigarettes or 50 cigars, 1 liter of alcohol, gifts up to BZ$400 value
entry_and_border.extension_info: 30-day extensions available from Belize Immigration for BZ$100. Can extend up to 6 months total.

health.malaria_risk: Low to moderate — present in rural and jungle areas, particularly southern Belize (Toledo district), Cayo district jungle areas, and some northern districts. Not a risk on the cayes or in Belize City.
health.malaria_prophylaxis_info: Recommended if spending significant time in Toledo district (southern jungle) or remote Cayo. Not needed for Ambergris Caye, Caye Caulker, or Belize City. Atovaquone/proguanil or doxycycline most commonly prescribed.
health.dengue_risk: Moderate — dengue present nationwide. More common in urban areas and lowlands. Use DEET repellent, especially during rainy season.
health.altitude_sickness_risk: None — Belize is entirely lowland. Highest point (Doyle's Delight) is only 1,124m.
health.food_water_safety: Tap water not recommended for drinking outside established hotels. Always use bottled or purified water. Food safety generally good in tourist areas — Belize has higher food hygiene standards than most Central American neighbors. Exercise normal caution with street food.
health.air_quality_concerns: Generally very good — low industrial activity. Seasonal smoke from agricultural burning in northern Belize (dry season). Belize City traffic minimal.
health.pharmacy_access: Available in Belize City, San Ignacio, San Pedro (Ambergris Caye), and Placencia. Limited on smaller cayes and in remote areas. Basic medications available OTC. Bring sufficient prescription medications.
health.medical_care_quality: Limited — Karl Heusner Memorial Hospital in Belize City offers basic care. Private clinics in Belize City and San Pedro. Very limited in rural and southern areas. Serious conditions require medical evacuation to the US or Mexico. Hyperbaric chamber on Ambergris Caye for dive emergencies.

risk_indicators: Belize City has significant gang-related violent crime
Parts of Belize City among the most dangerous in Central America per capita
Tourist areas (cayes, San Ignacio, Placencia) are genuinely safe
Hurricane risk on cayes (Category 4-5 storms historically devastating)
safety.crime_risk: Highly location-dependent — Belize City requires significant care (do not wander, use taxis, avoid southern neighborhoods). All major tourist destinations (Ambergris Caye, Caye Caulker, San Ignacio, Placencia, Hopkins) are safe and well-traveled. The vast majority of visitors have no safety issues.
safety.scam_risk: Low — occasional overcharging, inflated taxi fares in Belize City
safety.transport_safety: Water taxis between cayes safe and efficient. Domestic flights recommended over road travel for Belize City connections. Road travel between tourist towns (San Ignacio, Placencia, Hopkins) safe during daylight. Avoid Belize City street travel on foot.
safety.solo_female_travel_notes: Cayes and San Ignacio very manageable solo — English-speaking country makes communication easy. Belize City requires same precautions as any high-crime city. Beach towns like Hopkins and Placencia have relaxed, safe atmospheres. Night safety standard — stay in well-lit tourist areas.
safety.lgbtq_notes: Belize decriminalized same-sex relationships in 2019 (Supreme Court ruling). No same-sex marriage recognition. Social attitudes mixed — more conservative in rural and Mennonite communities, more tolerant in tourist areas and among younger urban Belizeans. Discretion recommended outside San Pedro and tourist-facing areas. No dedicated LGBTQ+ venues but generally no hostility in tourist areas.
safety.night_safety: Cayes (San Pedro, Caye Caulker) — safe at night in main town areas. San Ignacio — safe in downtown area at night. Placencia and Hopkins — safe at night. Belize City — exercise extreme caution at night, stay in hotel or use taxis only.
safe_areas: Caye Caulker (the Split and main village)
areas_to_avoid: Belize City — south side (Mesopotamia, Lake Independence, Port Loyola) extremely dangerous
Belize City — most areas on foot, especially at night
natural_disaster_risks: Hurricanes — cayes extremely vulnerable (Category 4 Dean 2007, Category 5 near-misses; 1961 Hurricane Hattie devastated Belize City)
Flooding — low-lying coastal and river areas
Storm surge — cayes have very little elevation above sea level
safety.political_stability_notes: Belize is one of the most politically stable countries in Central America — parliamentary democracy, peaceful transfers of power, and British Commonwealth membership. No significant political instability for tourists. Territorial dispute with Guatemala ongoing but no practical tourist impact.
common_scams: Taxi overcharging in Belize City — always confirm fare before getting in
Unofficial tour guides at ruins offering cheaper prices but poor service
Drug offers on Caye Caulker — be aware possession is illegal
Water taxi 'package deals' that include unnecessary add-ons
safety.police_reliability: Generally reliable in tourist areas — tourist police in San Pedro and major attractions. Belize Police Department responsive but under-resourced. Report incidents to tourist police or your embassy.

weather_and_seasonality.best_time_to_visit: November to April (dry season) — best diving visibility, clearest water, least rain. February-April driest and sunniest.
climate_zones: Northern lowlands (Corozal, Orange Walk) — subtropical, distinct dry season, sugarcane country
Belize City and cayes (Caribbean coast) — warm, humid, trade winds, distinct seasons
Cayo district (interior highlands) — slightly cooler, jungle, distinct wet/dry seasons
Southern Belize (Toledo, Stann Creek) — wettest area in Belize, near year-round rain, lush rainforest
Cayes and atolls — warm year-round, 25-30°C, most exposed to hurricanes
weather_and_seasonality.dry_season: November to April (north and central); southern Belize wetter year-round
weather_and_seasonality.hurricane_typhoon_season: June to November — Belize has direct Caribbean hurricane exposure. Cayes are extremely vulnerable — Hurricane Hattie (1961) obliterated Belize City, prompting move of capital to Belmopan.
weather_and_seasonality.dive_season: Year-round — visibility best November to June. July-October can have reduced visibility after rains. Great Blue Hole best November-April.

major_airports: Philip S. W. Goldson International Airport (BZE) — Belize City (only international airport, 16km from city)
Municipal Airstrip (TZA) — Belize City (domestic flights, closer to city)
airport_to_city_options: Taxi to Belize City ($25-35 USD, 20-30 min)
Water taxi connection to cayes via Marine Terminal (most popular option for caye-bound travelers)
Domestic flight from BZE to San Pedro or Placencia (15-20 min, $80-120 USD — highly recommended)
transport.public_transit_quality: Buses connect main towns on the Western and Southern highways — cheap and functional but slow. Not recommended for tourists with luggage or on tight schedules. No public transport on cayes.
transport.intercity_bus_quality: National Transport Lines and James Bus serve main routes (Belize City-San Ignacio, Belize City-Dangriga-Punta Gorda) — functional, air-conditioned coaches. Cheap (BZ$5-20) but slow. Reliable for the main highway routes.
transport.road_conditions: Western Highway (Belize City to San Ignacio) well-maintained. Southern Highway (Belmopan to Punta Gorda) paved but rough in sections. Hummingbird Highway (Belmopan to Dangriga) — some of the most scenic road in Belize, reasonably maintained. Many jungle lodge and ruin access roads unpaved.
four_x_four_needed_regions: Toledo district (remote Maya villages, rainy season)
transport.taxi_safety_notes: No ride-hailing apps in Belize. Taxis are unmetered — always confirm fare before getting in. Belize City taxis are safe but overcharge tourists. Green license plates indicate licensed taxis. Water taxis (Belize City to cayes) are safe, efficient, and the primary transport for caye visitors.
ferry_routes: Belize City (Marine Terminal) to Ambergris Caye / San Pedro (1.5 hrs, BZ$20-35 — San Pedro Belize Express, Caye Caulker Water Taxi)
Belize City to Caye Caulker (45 min, BZ$15-20)
Caye Caulker to Ambergris Caye (30 min)
Punta Gorda to Puerto Barrios, Guatemala (1.5 hrs — International ferry)
Inter-caye water taxis throughout the cayes
transport.border_crossing_notes: Main crossings: Santa Elena/Melchor de Mencos (Guatemala — Western Border, near San Ignacio); Chetumal/Subteniente López (Mexico — Northern Border, Corozal area). Belize is NOT part of CA-4 — separate entry stamp. Guatemala-Belize crossing is the most used by tourists (San Ignacio to Flores/Tikal route).

budget.daily_budget_backpacker: $50-80/day (Belize is notably more expensive than neighbors)
budget.dive_certification_avg: $350-500 (more expensive than Honduras but world-class reef)
budget.value_for_money: Below average for the region — Belize is the most expensive country in Central America. USD economy, English-speaking, and world-class dive sites mean prices approach Caribbean island levels. Budget travelers find it tough. Caye Caulker is notably cheaper than Ambergris Caye. Inland Belize (Cayo, Hopkins) is much better value.

connectivity.mobile_data_quality: Decent 4G in Belize City, San Pedro, and Caye Caulker. Moderate in San Ignacio and Placencia. Poor in remote jungle areas (Mountain Pine Ridge, Toledo), small cayes, and offshore atolls.
connectivity.wifi_quality: Good in San Pedro and most tourist-facing hotels, dive shops, and restaurants. Variable speeds. Caye Caulker and San Ignacio have good WiFi in hostels and cafes. Very limited in remote jungle lodges and small cayes.
best_remote_work_cities: San Pedro (Ambergris Caye) — best connectivity and infrastructure
San Ignacio — growing nomad presence, good WiFi in cafes
connectivity.power_reliability: Reliable in Belize City and San Pedro. Occasional outages on smaller cayes and in jungle areas. Bring power bank for remote lodges. Some jungle ecolodges use solar — power hours may be limited.

best_areas_to_stay: Ambergris Caye (San Pedro town) — most developed caye, best dive infrastructure, good restaurants and nightlife, wide price range
Caye Caulker — backpacker favorite, relaxed 'go slow' vibe, cheaper than Ambergris, smaller and more intimate
San Ignacio (Cayo district) — base for Maya ruins (Caracol, Xunantunich), jungle lodges, cave tubing, and the western highlands
Placencia — southern beach town, mix of budget and upscale, laid-back Caribbean vibe, good whale shark season
Hopkins — small Garifuna village on the southern coast, authentic culture, less touristy than Placencia, good budget options
Punta Gorda — base for Toledo district, deep southern Belize, Maya village stays, very off-the-beaten-path
Turneffe Atoll / Lighthouse Reef — live-aboard diving and exclusive island resorts near the Great Blue Hole
Mountain Pine Ridge (Cayo) — jungle lodges, waterfalls, cool highland climate — some of Belize's finest ecolodges
nightlife_areas: San Pedro (Ambergris Caye) — Fido's, Jaguar's Temple, Secret Beach
accommodation.peak_booking_season: December to April; Easter week; Lobster Fest (San Pedro — June) and other festivals
accommodation.accommodation_notes: Ambergris Caye is Belize's most visited destination — wide range from backpacker hostels to luxury resorts. Book well ahead December-April. Caye Caulker is the budget alternative — smaller, more relaxed, 'go slow' is the official motto. San Ignacio is the best value hub for jungle and ruins. Mountain Pine Ridge jungle lodges (Blancaneaux Lodge, Hidden Valley Inn) are world-class ecolodges.

local_customs: Belize is uniquely multicultural — Creole, Garifuna, Maya, Mestizo, Mennonite, and expat communities each with distinct culture
Belizean Creole is warm, colorful, and musical — learning a few phrases is welcomed
'Go slow' is the unofficial motto of Caye Caulker — patience and relaxation are expected
Garifuna culture (Hopkins, Dangriga) is deeply proud — drumming, punta music, and cassava-based food are central
Mennonite communities (Spanish Lookout, Shipyard) are conservative — dress modestly if visiting, no photography without permission
Maya communities in Toledo prefer respectful, quiet visitors — remove shoes before entering homes
culture.dress_expectations: Casual beach and resort wear throughout. Modest clothing in villages, churches, and Mennonite communities. Swimwear only at beaches and pool areas. Light clothing year-round on cayes.
culture.religious_sensitivity: Mix of Christianity (Catholic, Protestant, evangelical), Maya spiritualism, and Garifuna religious traditions. Religion generally low-key for tourists. Garifuna Dugu ceremonies are sacred — attend only if invited.
social_norms: Belizeans are warm and direct — English makes interaction easy and natural
The laid-back Caribbean pace is genuine — rushing is culturally jarring
Belizean pride in their reef, jungles, and multicultural identity is genuine — engage with enthusiasm
Garifuna pride is fierce — learn about their history (survivors of British colonial resistance on St. Vincent)
Lobster is a major cultural and economic touchstone — lobster season (July 15 to February 28) is celebrated
culture.greeting_style: Informal and warm — handshake or nod, 'What's up?' or 'Alright?' in Creole. First names used quickly. Very friendly to English-speaking tourists.
culture.public_behavior_notes: PDA acceptable in tourist areas. Modest in villages and traditional communities. Keep valuables low-profile in Belize City.
culture.photography_etiquette: Ask permission in indigenous Maya villages and Garifuna communities. Mennonite communities generally decline photography. Ruins and natural sites freely photographable. Underwater photography — check dive operator rules.
important_holidays: Garifuna Settlement Day (November 19) — national holiday celebrating Garifuna arrival in Belize, huge celebrations in Dangriga and Hopkins with drumming and punta
Baron Bliss Day (March 9) — honors Henry Edward Ernest Victor Bliss, British fisherman who left his fortune to Belize
Lobster Fest (San Pedro, Placencia — June/July) — celebration of lobster season opening
legal_restrictions_tourists_often_miss: Coral collection is illegal — even dead coral or shells. Heavy fines.
Drone permits required — Civil Aviation Department authorization needed
Spearfishing restricted — prohibited in many areas, especially near the reef. Check local rules.
Lobster season strictly enforced — fishing or purchasing out of season illegal (March 1 to July 14)
Conch harvesting regulated — check current rules
Marijuana — despite Caribbean vibes, possession is illegal. Decriminalization of small amounts proposed but not yet in effect as of 2026.

signature_dishes: Rice and beans — the national staple; red kidney beans cooked with coconut milk and served with rice; appears at nearly every Belizean meal as the base
Stew chicken — slow-cooked chicken in recado (achiote) sauce; served with rice and beans; the quintessential Belizean comfort meal
Hudut — Garifuna dish of fish (snapper or barracuda) in a rich coconut broth with plantain mash (fufu); one of the most distinctive dishes in Central America
Sere — another Garifuna fish and coconut soup, lighter than hudut
Garnaches — fried corn tortillas topped with beans, cabbage, and cheese; street snack in northern Belize
Panades — fried corn masa pockets stuffed with fish or beans; popular street food in Belize City
Johnny cake — soft, slightly sweet baked bread rolls; the Belizean breakfast staple, served with eggs and beans
Fry jacks — deep-fried dough puffs served with eggs, beans, and cheese for breakfast; beloved national breakfast item
Conch fritters — battered and fried queen conch; quintessential caye snack
Conch ceviche — raw conch marinated in lime, cilantro, onion, and chile; best on the cayes
Lobster — the prized seasonal delicacy (July 15 to February 28); grilled, in pasta, in burgers, or thermidor at upscale restaurants
Gibnut (paca) — large rodent considered a delicacy; gained fame as 'royal rat' when served to Queen Elizabeth II in 1985
food.street_food_safety: Generally safe — panades, garnaches, and Johnny cakes from busy street vendors are reliable. High standards compared to neighbors. Caye Caulker and San Pedro have excellent street food scenes.
food.vegetarian_friendliness: Moderate — rice and beans is naturally vegetarian. Bean-based dishes widespread. Growing vegetarian options in San Pedro and San Ignacio catering to international travelers. Limited in traditional Garifuna and Maya villages.
food.vegan_friendliness: Limited but improving — coconut-based Garifuna food can be vegan-friendly. Dedicated vegan options growing in San Pedro. Challenging in rural areas.
must_try_drinks: Belikin beer — the only beer brewed in Belize, ubiquitous and cold; the national drink in every sense
One Barrel rum — Belizean rum, widely consumed locally
Rum punch — fruit juice and rum; caye bar staple
Seaweed shake — ground seaweed blended with milk, vanilla, cinnamon, and nutmeg; surprisingly delicious Belizean health drink
Horchata — rice and cinnamon cold drink, more Latin influence in western Belize
Fresh coconut water — from green coconuts on the cayes
Cashew wine — fermented cashew fruit; seasonal specialty in Cayo district
Soursop juice — fresh tropical fruit juice
Rum cream (One Barrel) — local cream liqueur
regional_specialties: Northern Belize (Corozal, Orange Walk) — Mestizo-influenced; tamales, escabeche (sour onion chicken soup), relleno negro (black turkey stew with charred chile)
Cayes (San Pedro, Caye Caulker) — fresh seafood dominant; lobster, conch, snapper, ceviche, rum-based cocktails
Garifuna south (Hopkins, Dangriga) — hudut, sere, cassava bread, coconut-heavy cuisine; some of the most distinctive food in the country
Cayo district (San Ignacio) — Maya and Mestizo influenced; mountain chicken (frog legs — now protected), pibil (pit-roasted pork), tamales
Toledo district (Punta Gorda) — Q'eqchi' and Mopan Maya traditional foods; corn-based dishes, caldo (broth), wild game
food.drinking_water_notes: Do not drink tap water. Bottled water widely available throughout tourist areas. Many hotels provide filtered water. On remote cayes and jungle lodges — always confirm water source.
food.alcohol_rules: Legal drinking age 18. No strict public drinking laws. Alcohol widely available.

top_national_parks: Great Blue Hole / Lighthouse Reef Atoll — UNESCO World Heritage (part of Belize Barrier Reef) — world-famous 300m-wide, 125m-deep marine sinkhole
Belize Barrier Reef Reserve System — UNESCO World Heritage — second largest barrier reef in the world
Caracol Archaeological Reserve — largest Maya site in Belize, deep in the jungle
Cockscomb Basin Wildlife Sanctuary — world's first jaguar preserve
Blue Hole National Park (inland) — turquoise limestone pool in the jungle (different from the marine Great Blue Hole)
Five Blues Lake National Park — color-shifting lake in Cayo
Half Moon Caye Natural Monument — UNESCO, nesting site for red-footed booby
Crooked Tree Wildlife Sanctuary — lagoon birdwatching, jabiru stork
Rio Bravo Conservation Area — 100,000+ hectares of pristine jungle
Shipstern Nature Reserve (Corozal) — tropical forest and lagoon
Glover's Reef Atoll — UNESCO, remote coral atoll, excellent diving

top_day_hikes.name: ATM Cave (Actun Tunichil Muknal)
top_day_hikes.description: One of the most extraordinary archaeological experiences in the world — swim and hike through an active river cave system to reach a Maya ceremonial chamber filled with intact skeletal remains and ceramic offerings left 1,000-1,500 years ago. The iconic 'Crystal Maiden' calcite-encrusted skeleton lies undisturbed exactly where Maya priests left her. National Geographic named it one of the Top 10 Sacred Sites on Earth.
top_day_hikes.notes: Access from San Ignacio, 45 min drive + 45 min jungle hike. Licensed guide mandatory — cannot visit independently. Entry fee $25 USD. Photography now prohibited inside the cave (cameras banned after tourist accidentally dropped camera on a skull). Cameras not allowed at all — experience it fully present. Involves swimming in river, climbing through passages, and wading. Extraordinary.

top_day_hikes.name: Caracol Maya Ruins
top_day_hikes.description: Explore the largest Maya archaeological site in Belize — Caracol at its peak had a population larger than modern Belize City and dominated Tikal in the Maya Classic period. Caana (Sky Palace) is the tallest man-made structure in Belize at 43m. Deep jungle setting with howler monkeys, toucans, and tapir tracks.
top_day_hikes.notes: 50 miles from San Ignacio on rough jungle road — requires 4WD or organized tour. Access restricted after 3pm (armed military escort only). Best visited with licensed tour operator from San Ignacio ($80-120 including guide). Allow full day. Combine with Mountain Pine Ridge waterfalls on same trip.

top_day_hikes.name: Xunantunich Maya Ruins
top_day_hikes.description: Well-preserved hilltop Maya ceremonial center with magnificent frieze-decorated El Castillo pyramid (40m) with panoramic views over the Mopan River valley into Guatemala. One of the most visually impressive sites in Belize. Howler monkeys in the canopy overhead.
top_day_hikes.notes: 8 miles from San Ignacio, accessed by hand-cranked cable ferry across the Mopan River (free, charmingly old-fashioned). Entry BZ$10. Can hike to top of El Castillo for spectacular valley views. Easy half-day trip. Combine with Cahal Pech ruins (in San Ignacio town itself) for a full ruins day.

top_day_hikes.name: Cockscomb Basin Jaguar Preserve Hike
top_day_hikes.description: Hike through the world's first jaguar sanctuary — a 400km² watershed preserve protecting the highest density jaguar population in Central America. Multiple trail options from short nature walks to multi-day backcountry routes. Excellent birdwatching, waterfall swimming, and the ever-present possibility of jaguar tracks (the cats themselves are elusive).
top_day_hikes.notes: Based from Cockscomb Basin visitor center, accessible from Southern Highway. Entry BZ$10. No guides required for main trails but recommended for backcountry. Jaguar sightings very rare — mostly tracks, scrapes, and camera traps. Ben's Bluff trail has excellent mountain views. Antelope Falls and Tiger Fern trail both excellent.

top_day_hikes.name: Inland Blue Hole and St. Herman's Cave
top_day_hikes.description: Visit Belize's inland Blue Hole — a stunning 25m-diameter, 6m-deep turquoise sinkhole pool in the jungle, perfect for swimming. Nearby St. Herman's Cave is one of the most impressive entrance chambers in Belize — enormous and eerie. The two sites combined in Belize's smallest national park.
top_day_hikes.notes: On Hummingbird Highway, 45 min from Belmopan. Entry BZ$8. Blue Hole swimming is excellent — clear and very cold. St. Herman's Cave — go with a guide for the best experience (guides available at entrance). Easy half-day trip from San Ignacio or en route south.

top_day_hikes.name: Cave Tubing (Nohoch Che'en / Caves Branch)
top_day_hikes.description: Float through a series of ancient river caves on an inner tube with a headlamp — one of Belize's most popular and enjoyable tourist activities. The Caves Branch River flows through a network of Maya ceremonial caves, past stalactites and stalagmites. Accessible, fun for all ages and fitness levels.
best_months: Year-round (avoid extreme rainy season peak — river can run too high)
top_day_hikes.notes: Most popular day trip from San Ignacio or Belize City. Tours from $40-60 including transport and guide. Inner tube and headlamp provided. Combine with ziplining at the same site (Jaguar Paw and Caves Branch Resort are main operators). Can be combined with inland Blue Hole on same day.

top_day_hikes.name: Great Blue Hole Dive / Snorkel
top_day_hikes.duration: Full day (boat tour from Ambergris Caye or Caye Caulker)
top_day_hikes.description: Dive or snorkel the world-famous Great Blue Hole — a perfectly circular marine sinkhole 300m wide and 125m deep, formed during the last ice age. Famous for Caribbean reef sharks, nurse sharks, midnight parrotfish, and the extraordinary walls dropping into the deep blue. UNESCO World Heritage. Consistently on every 'best dive sites in the world' list.
top_day_hikes.notes: Full-day boat trip ($250-350 including 3 dives at Blue Hole, Half Moon Caye, and Long Caye). Departs from San Pedro or Caye Caulker 5:30-6am, returns evening. The Blue Hole itself is best for divers (stalactites visible 40m+ deep) — snorkelers see the circle from the surface and may be disappointed by the lack of reef fish. The surrounding atolls are excellent for snorkelers. Seasickness precautions recommended — rough open-ocean crossing.

top_day_hikes.name: Crooked Tree Wildlife Sanctuary Boat Tour
top_day_hikes.region: Belize district (Northern Belize, 33 miles north of Belize City)
top_day_hikes.description: Boat through a vast freshwater lagoon system — the nesting ground for the jabiru stork, the largest flying bird in the Western Hemisphere. Hundreds of species of waterfowl, herons, egrets, spoonbills, ospreys, and black-collared hawks. One of the finest birdwatching experiences in Belize.
top_day_hikes.notes: Entry BZ$8. Hire a local guide from Crooked Tree village for boat tour ($40-60/boat). Jabiru storks nest December-May — best birdwatching February-May. Accessible from Northern Highway on the way to or from Chetumal/Mexico border. Can be done as day trip from Belize City.

multi_day_treks.name: Toledo Maya Village Experience
multi_day_treks.region: Toledo district (southern Belize — Punta Gorda area)
multi_day_treks.description: Stay in Q'eqchi' and Mopan Maya villages in the remote Toledo district — one of the most authentic cultural immersion experiences in Central America. Community guesthouses, traditional food, cacao farm tours, waterfall hikes, and living Maya culture far from the tourist trail.
multi_day_treks.notes: Toledo Ecotourism Association (TEA) coordinates village stays ($BZ50-75/night including meals). Punta Gorda is the base — fly or take bus from Belize City. Remote and wet — this is Belize's rainiest area. Worth the effort for extraordinary authentic experience. Cacao and chocolate tours excellent.

multi_day_treks.name: Lamanai Archaeological Reserve and River Journey
multi_day_treks.description: Boat up the New River Lagoon through crocodile-filled waters to reach Lamanai — one of Belize's most impressive Maya sites, continuously inhabited for over 3,000 years. The High Temple (33m) overlooks the lagoon. Howler monkeys, jabiru storks, black orchids, and morelet's crocodiles are all present. Most atmospheric site approach in Belize.
multi_day_treks.notes: River boat departs from Orange Walk town ($50-80 per person including guide). Stay overnight in the area for early morning site visit. Lamanai has modest on-site accommodation. Entry BZ$10. The river journey itself — past crocodiles, birds, and river vegetation — is as memorable as the ruins. Sugar cane country surrounds.

waterfalls: Rio On Pools (Mountain Pine Ridge) — granite boulders and tiered cascade pools, excellent swimming
Hidden Valley Falls (Mountain Pine Ridge) — 1,600 ft drop, one of the highest in Central America, best viewed from a distance during rainy season
Five Sisters Falls (Mountain Pine Ridge) — five-strand waterfall at Gaia River Lodge
Antelope Falls (Cockscomb Basin) — jungle waterfall in jaguar preserve
lakes: Crooked Tree Lagoon — freshwater birding paradise, jabiru stork
New River Lagoon — access to Lamanai ruins, crocodiles
Lake Yaxhá (near border) — some cross-border tourism from Guatemala side
Five Blues Lake — color-shifting karst lake in Cayo
rivers: New River — boat journey to Lamanai, crocodile habitat
Mopan River — San Ignacio area, tubing and kayaking
Macal River — San Ignacio area, kayaking and riverside lodges
Moho River — Toledo district, remote jungle
wildlife: Jaguar (Cockscomb Basin — highest density in world; rarely seen)
Tapir — national animal; present in jungle areas
Baird's tapir (Cockscomb, Cayo jungle)
Howler monkey (nationwide — very audible at Lamanai, Caracol, Xunantunich)
Spider monkey (Cayo jungle, Cockscomb)
Morelet's crocodile (New River, Crooked Tree — endemic to Belize/Guatemala/Mexico)
American crocodile (coastal and river areas)
Manatee (Corozal Bay, Southern Lagoon — Belize has one of the healthiest manatee populations in the Caribbean)
Jabiru stork (Crooked Tree — largest flying bird in Western Hemisphere)
Scarlet macaw (Red Bank village, Cayo — largest scarlet macaw roosting site in Belize)
Keel-billed toucan — national bird, widespread
Red-footed booby (Half Moon Caye — large nesting colony)
Magnificent frigatebird (cayes)
Whale shark (Gladden Spit — seasonal, March-June)
Caribbean reef shark (Great Blue Hole, atolls)
Nurse shark (Shark Ray Alley, Hol Chan — habituated to snorkelers)
Southern stingray (Shark Ray Alley)
Loggerhead sea turtle (barrier reef nesting)
Hawksbill sea turtle (barrier reef)
Green sea turtle (barrier reef)
West Indian manatee (Corozal Bay, southern lagoons)
birdwatching_hotspots: Crooked Tree Wildlife Sanctuary — jabiru stork, 285+ species, best February-May
Lamanai ruins and New River — river and forest birds from the boat
Cockscomb Basin — forest interior species, toucans, parrots
Shipstern Nature Reserve (Corozal) — rare forest birds, manatees
Chan Chich Lodge (Orange Walk) — 350+ species, private jungle lodge birding
Red Bank village (Stann Creek) — scarlet macaw roosting congregation November-March
Caracol / Mountain Pine Ridge — highland pine forest species different from lowland jungle
Lighthouse Reef / Half Moon Caye — seabirds, red-footed booby nesting colony
stargazing_spots: Mountain Pine Ridge (Cayo) — dark skies, highland elevation
Remote cayes and atolls (Glover's Reef, Turneffe) — zero light pollution over the Caribbean
Cockscomb Basin — deep jungle, no light pollution
Chan Chich Lodge area (Orange Walk) — remote jungle, exceptional dark skies
scenic_drives: Hummingbird Highway (Belmopan to Dangriga) — most scenic road in Belize through Maya Mountains, citrus farms, and jungle
Western Highway (Belize City to San Ignacio) — Belize River valley and western highlands
Southern Highway (Dangriga to Punta Gorda) — remote, tropical, beautiful
Mountain Pine Ridge Road (San Ignacio to Caracol) — pine forest to jungle transition

dive_and_snorkel_sites.name: Great Blue Hole
dive_and_snorkel_sites.description: The world's most famous dive site — a perfectly circular UNESCO World Heritage marine sinkhole 300m wide, surrounded by a ring of coral. Caribbean reef sharks, bull sharks (seasonal), nurse sharks, and midnight parrotfish patrolling the walls. Stalactites at 40m+ prove this was once a dry cave during the Ice Age. Humbling scale — the walls drop into absolute darkness.
dive_and_snorkel_sites.notes: 50 miles offshore — full-day boat journey from San Pedro. $250-350 full-day trip includes 3 dives. Best for advanced divers. Snorkelers see the circle from the surface. One of the world's great bucket-list dive experiences.

dive_and_snorkel_sites.name: Hol Chan Marine Reserve / Shark Ray Alley
dive_and_snorkel_sites.region: Ambergris Caye (3 miles south of San Pedro)
dive_and_snorkel_sites.description: Belize's most visited marine reserve — a natural channel cut through the barrier reef teeming with fish life. Shark Ray Alley is an adjacent sand flat where nurse sharks and southern stingrays congregate in extraordinary numbers — snorkelers can swim directly among dozens of them. One of the best accessible marine wildlife experiences in the Caribbean.
dive_and_snorkel_sites.notes: 15-20 min boat ride from San Pedro. BZ$20 marine reserve fee. Multiple snorkel and dive operators. Nurse sharks are habituated and non-aggressive. Can be crowded (most popular site in Belize). Morning sessions less busy. Easy half-day trip.

dive_and_snorkel_sites.name: Turneffe Atoll
dive_and_snorkel_sites.region: Turneffe Islands (25 miles east of Belize City)
dive_and_snorkel_sites.description: The largest atoll in the Western Hemisphere — 50km of pristine wall and reef diving, elbow reef with strong currents and bull shark encounters, manatee-inhabited lagoons, and remote island camps. Less visited than the Blue Hole corridor. One of the finest all-around atoll diving destinations in the Caribbean.
dive_and_snorkel_sites.notes: Day trips from Belize City or multi-day stays at Turneffe Island Resort and Turneffe Flats. Elbow Reef (south end) is the signature site — bull sharks, eagle rays, and Caribbean reef sharks in strong currents. Manatees visible in calm lagoon areas.

dive_and_snorkel_sites.name: Gladden Spit (Whale Shark Site)
dive_and_snorkel_sites.description: One of the world's most reliable whale shark encounter sites — whale sharks aggregate here during full moons March-June to feed on spawning cubera snapper. Filter-feeding giants up to 12m long in open water. An extraordinary bucket-list marine wildlife experience.
dive_and_snorkel_sites.notes: Access from Placencia (2.5 hr boat ride). Full-moon trips only during shark season ($150-200). Strict no-touch rules — must maintain 3m distance. Water can be murky from fish spawn. Open-water dive in the blue — some current. Completely worth it for the experience.

dive_and_snorkel_sites.name: Glover's Reef Atoll
dive_and_snorkel_sites.description: UNESCO World Heritage — the most remote and pristine atoll in Belize. Exceptional wall and reef diving, world-class fly fishing on the flats, and some of the clearest water in the Caribbean. Very few visitors — most who come stay at the handful of remote island camps.
dive_and_snorkel_sites.notes: 2-3 hour boat from Dangriga or Sittee River. Remote island camps (Island Expeditions, Off the Wall) offer multi-day packages including diving and kayaking. Excellent sea kayaking between reef formations inside the lagoon. Not easily done as a day trip.

wildlife_zones.name: Cockscomb Basin Wildlife Sanctuary
wildlife_zones.notes: 400+ km² of protected watershed — the highest jaguar density in the world. Jaguars rarely seen but tracks, scrapes, and camera traps are frequent. Multiple hiking trails. Entry BZ$10. Based from Maya Centre village. Combine with Hopkins beach stay.

wildlife_zones.name: Crooked Tree Wildlife Sanctuary
key_species: Jabiru stork (Western Hemisphere's largest flying bird)
wildlife_zones.notes: Freshwater lagoon system — best jabiru stork viewing in the world during February-May nesting season. Hire local guides from Crooked Tree village. BZ$8 entry.

wildlife_zones.name: Corozal Bay / Southern Lagoon Manatee Habitat
wildlife_zones.notes: Belize has one of the Caribbean's most important manatee populations. Corozal Bay and Southern Lagoon (Gales Point) are key habitats. Gales Point Manatee community offers boat tours to see manatees in the wild.

wildlife_zones.name: Barrier Reef and Atolls Marine Ecosystem
wildlife_zones.notes: UNESCO World Heritage. Second largest barrier reef in the world. The entire reef system from Bacalar Chico in the north to Sapodilla Cayes in the south is a world-class marine ecosystem. Healthy reef, good visibility, and accessible from multiple base points.

sports_and_activities.name: Scuba Diving
sports_and_activities.notes: Belize is THE dive destination of the Caribbean mainland — the barrier reef and three atolls offer world-class diversity from beginner-friendly shallow reef to advanced wall and blue-water diving. Great Blue Hole is on every serious diver's bucket list. PADI certifications widely available ($350-500).

sports_and_activities.name: Snorkeling
best_regions: Shark Ray Alley / Hol Chan (San Pedro)
sports_and_activities.notes: Shark Ray Alley is the single most popular tourist experience in Belize — nurse sharks and stingrays at arm's reach. Caye Caulker has excellent patch reef snorkeling directly off the island. No diving certification needed — one of the best snorkeling destinations in the Caribbean.

sports_and_activities.name: Cave Exploration (Caving and Cave Tubing)
sports_and_activities.notes: Belize has exceptional cave tourism — ATM (Actun Tunichil Muknal) is arguably the single most spectacular tourist experience in the country. Barton Creek Cave is floated by canoe through a dripping limestone cavern with Maya pottery and burial sites. A uniquely Belizean experience.

sports_and_activities.name: Maya Archaeology
best_months: Year-round (dry season best for jungle sites)
sports_and_activities.notes: Belize has hundreds of documented Maya sites — fewer than 40 are properly excavated. The jungle-buried ruins at Caracol and Lamanai are extraordinary. Xunantunich is the most accessible from San Ignacio. Toledo's Lubaantun and Nim Li Punit are rarely visited gems.

sports_and_activities.name: Fly Fishing
sports_and_activities.notes: Belize is a world-class fly fishing destination — permit, tarpon, and bonefish in the Caribbean flats. Turneffe Flats and Glover's Reef offer some of the best permit fishing anywhere. Specialized lodges cater to serious fly fishing travelers. Catch and release standard.

sports_and_activities.name: Kayaking and River Tubing
sports_and_activities.notes: Glover's Reef offers world-class sea kayaking through a pristine atoll lagoon. Island Expeditions and Slickrock Adventures offer multi-day kayak camping expeditions. San Ignacio river tubing on the Mopan is leisurely and enjoyable.

sports_and_activities.name: Whale Shark Swimming
sports_and_activities.notes: One of the world's most reliable whale shark encounters — aggregating to feed on spawning snapper during full moons. Full moon boat trips from Placencia. Strictly regulated ($150-200/person). An extraordinary wildlife experience.

sports_and_activities.name: Birdwatching
sports_and_activities.notes: Belize has 600+ recorded bird species in a tiny country — exceptional diversity per square kilometer. The jabiru stork at Crooked Tree and the scarlet macaw congregation at Red Bank are world-class wildlife spectacles. Chan Chich Lodge is renowned as one of the finest jungle birding lodges in the Americas.

sports_and_activities.name: Garifuna Cultural Experiences
best_months: Year-round; November 19 (Garifuna Settlement Day) most spectacular
sports_and_activities.notes: The Garifuna people — descended from African and Arawak/Carib communities — have one of the most distinctive cultures in the Americas. Hopkins is the most accessible and welcoming Garifuna village. Drumming lessons, punta dancing, cassava bread making, and hudut cooking classes all available. Garifuna Settlement Day (November 19) is an extraordinary national celebration.

top_regions: Ambergris Caye and the barrier reef
Cayo district (San Ignacio, Mountain Pine Ridge, Caracol)
The Atolls (Lighthouse Reef, Turneffe, Glover's Reef)

sample_itineraries.one_week: Belize City (transit) → Ambergris Caye or Caye Caulker (3 days diving/snorkeling) → San Ignacio (3 days — ATM Cave, Xunantunich, cave tubing)
sample_itineraries.two_weeks: Belize City (transit) → Ambergris Caye (3 days, Great Blue Hole day trip) → Caye Caulker (1 day) → San Ignacio (3 days — ATM Cave, Caracol, Mountain Pine Ridge) → Hopkins (2 days — Garifuna culture, Cockscomb Basin) → Placencia (2 days — snorkeling, Gladden Spit if in season)
sample_itineraries.classic_route: Belize City → Ambergris Caye → Caye Caulker → San Ignacio → Caracol → ATM Cave
sample_itineraries.offbeat_route: Corozal → Lamanai river journey → Crooked Tree birdwatching → Cockscomb jaguar hike → Toledo Maya village stays → Glover's Reef sea kayaking

practical_info.arrival_tips: Philip Goldson Airport (BZE) is 16km from Belize City. Most tourists DON'T stay in Belize City — take a taxi directly to the Marine Terminal for a water taxi to the cayes, or a domestic flight to San Pedro, Placencia, or Punta Gorda. Pre-book connecting transport. Belize City is a transit point, not a destination for most visitors.
practical_info.airport_scam_notes: Taxi touts at the airport are persistent — confirm fare before getting in ($25-35 to city). No Uber in Belize. Domestic flight check-in at Tropic Air or Maya Island Air counters is straightforward.
practical_info.local_sim_where_to_buy: BTL (Digicell) SIM at the airport or throughout Belize City. Smart (Speednet) for secondary option. $10-20 for data. Bring passport.
practical_info.money_exchange_notes: USD accepted everywhere at fixed 2:1 rate — no exchange needed for American travelers. ATMs dispense BZD. Always confirm whether prices quoted are BZD or USD — locals sometimes say 'dollars' meaning BZD.

language_helpful_phrases.hello: Hey / What's up (Creole: 'Weh di go on?')
language_helpful_phrases.no_problem: No problem (Creole: 'Noh worry') — very common response

laws.drug_laws_strictness: Strict — possession illegal despite Caribbean tourist vibe. Marijuana decriminalization of small amounts (10g) in personal possession proposed/partially implemented — verify current status. Public smoking illegal. Do not purchase from strangers.
laws.alcohol_laws: Legal drinking age 18. No strict public drinking laws. Belikin beer can be consumed on the beach in most areas.
laws.drone_rules: Permits required from Civil Aviation Department. Prohibited over archaeological sites (Caracol, Lamanai, etc.), national parks, airports, military areas, and near the barrier reef without special permit. Apply weeks in advance.
laws.wild_camping_rules: Permitted in some areas with landowner permission. Prohibited in national parks and wildlife sanctuaries without special permit.
laws.protected_area_rules: Entry fees BZ$8-25 at major sites. Coral collection absolutely prohibited — even dead coral or shells. Heavy fines and possible prosecution. Spearfishing prohibited in most reef areas. No littering in marine reserves. Follow all guide instructions.
laws.photography_laws: No special restrictions for tourists. Photography of government buildings, military installations, and police operations should be avoided. Always ask permission in indigenous communities and Garifuna villages.
laws.wildlife_laws: Strict — export of pre-Columbian artifacts completely prohibited (criminal offense). Coral, queen conch, and lobster (out of season) collection illegal. Manatee harassment illegal — maintain distance. Crocodile disturbance illegal. Spearfishing regulated. Wildlife removal from protected areas a serious offense.
laws.lgbtq_legal_status: Legal — Belize Supreme Court decriminalized same-sex relations in 2019 (confirmed on appeal 2023). No same-sex marriage recognition. No legal partnership protections. Discrimination protections limited.
other_important_laws: Lobster season strictly enforced — purchasing, fishing, or possessing out of season (March 1 - July 14) illegal
Queen conch harvesting regulated — check current quotas
Pre-Columbian artifact removal from any site is a criminal offense
No coral collection of any kind — dead or alive
Firearms strictly controlled — tourists cannot bring firearms without prior authorization
Cash over $10,000 USD must be declared on entry and exit

family_travel.car_seat_rules: Required by law but rarely enforced. Bring your own.
family_activities: Shark Ray Alley snorkeling — nurse sharks and stingrays at arm's length (age 6+ comfortable swimmers)
Cave tubing at Caves Branch — inner tube float through jungle caves, easy and fun for kids 8+
Xunantunich Maya ruins — climbable pyramid with howler monkeys overhead; short easy hike
Lamanai river boat tour — crocodiles, monkeys, and toucans from the boat; educational and thrilling for all ages
Belize Zoo — world's finest zoo using only rescued native Belizean animals; tapirs, jaguars, harpy eagles — extraordinary
Crocodile sighting on the New River — all ages
Caye Caulker beach and snorkeling — gentle, small island, very safe
Ambergris Caye glass-bottom boat — see the reef without getting wet
Chocolate and cacao farm tours (Cayo and Toledo) — hands-on and educational
Inland Blue Hole swimming — cool turquoise pool in the jungle
family_travel.notes: The Belize Zoo (on the Western Highway near Belize City) is genuinely one of the finest zoos in the Western Hemisphere — worth a stop with children of all ages. ATM Cave is NOT suitable for children under 10 or those uncomfortable with confined spaces and swimming in dark water.

accessibility.wheelchair_accessibility: Very limited throughout. Cayes (San Pedro and Caye Caulker) involve sand paths, docks, and water taxis — difficult for wheelchair users. Ruins (Caracol, Lamanai, Xunantunich) have steep, uneven terrain. Belize City has some paved sidewalks. Upscale resorts on Ambergris Caye have the best accessibility infrastructure in the country.
accessibility.accessible_transport: No wheelchair-accessible public transport. Private taxis and resort shuttles may accommodate with advance notice. Water taxis require boarding from docks — difficult without assistance.
accessible_attractions: Belize Zoo (Western Highway) — partially accessible with paved paths through most areas
Some upscale Ambergris Caye resorts with beach accessibility (partial)
Lamanai visitor center and some flat areas (partial)
Placencia beachfront boardwalk — mostly flat, partially accessible

gear.cold_weather_gear_notes: Not needed — Belize is warm year-round. A light long-sleeve layer for air-conditioned boats and restaurants is useful. Cayes have pleasant trade wind breezes.
outdoor_gear_shops: Dive gear (BCD, regulator, wetsuit, fins) rented at all dive shops in San Pedro, Caye Caulker, Placencia — $15-30/day for full kit
Snorkel gear rental everywhere on the cayes — $5-10/day
Cave tubing gear (tubes, headlamps, life jackets) included in tour price
Kayaks rentable at most beach resorts and Glover's Reef camps
Very limited dry-land outdoor gear retail — bring good hiking boots and rain gear from home
SPF 50+ reef-safe sunscreen available in San Pedro but expensive — bring from home
Reef-safe sunscreen strongly recommended and required in some protected areas
gear.special_notes: Reef-safe sunscreen (mineral-based, no oxybenzone or octinoxate) is strongly recommended and required in some marine reserve areas. Regular chemical sunscreen is harmful to coral and actively damages the reef Belize depends on — please use reef-safe alternatives. ATM Cave requires closed-toe shoes that can get wet.
